Chinnici discusses Calpurnius Siculus' allusions to Vergil. Gärtner 2008 observes that Statius' epilogue alludes to Lucan 9.980-86 and thus imitates Vergil; Gärtner 2010 argues that the structure of Silius' Punica alludes in several ways to the structure of the Aeneid. Gasti is concerned with Faustus of Riez (ca. ad 405-ca. 490). Hulls shows that Ammianus5 unusual phrase "raising the standards of his obstinacy" at 14.1.10, describing the Caesar Gallus, is framed by a double allusion to Aen. 2.304-7 and 2.496-99 and must be a reference to a description of the emperor Domitian at Statius, Silv. 4.2. Magnelli discusses Calpurnius Siculus' main models, Theocritus and Vergil. Marshall demonstrates that Statius, Silv. 1.1 alludes to epic, and to the Aeneid in particular. Mazzucco touches on women's writing of Vergilian centones. Risi shows that Silius5 character Podaetus is a literary invention which owes much to Vergil's Achilles and to the Achilles of Statius' Achilleid. Van Mal-Maeder examines Endelechius' fourth-century ad poem De mortibus boum and discusses its debt to Vergil's Eclogues and Georgics , which are themselves indebted to Lucretius. Vieira discusses Lucan's dependence on Aen. 8.190-272. Borghini offers the conjecture "et matan uitatas umbras" at Petronius
62.7 on the basis of Aen. 6.290-94. Chinnici discusses Calpurnius Siculus'
allusions to Vergil. Gärtner 2008 observes that Statius' epilogue alludes
to Lucan 9.980-86 and thus imitates Vergil; Gärtner 2010 argues that
the structure of Silius' Punica alludes in several ways to the structure of
the Aeneid. Gasti is concerned with Faustus of Riez (ca. ad 405-ca. 490).
Hulls shows that Ammianus5 unusual phrase "raising the standards of
his obstinacy" at 14.1.10, describing the Caesar Gallus, is framed by a
double allusion to Aen. 2.304-7 and 2.496-99 and must be a reference
to a description of the emperor Domitian at Statius, Silv. 4.2. Magnelli
discusses Calpurnius Siculus' main models, Theocritus and Vergil.
Marshall demonstrates that Statius, Silv. 1.1 alludes to epic, and to the
Aeneid in particular. Mazzucco touches on women's writing of Vergilian
centones. Risi shows that Silius5 character Podaetus is a literary invention
which owes much to Vergil's Achilles and to the Achilles of Statius'
Achilleid. Van Mal-Maeder examines Endelechius' fourth-century ad poem
De mortibus boum and discusses its debt to Vergil's Eclogues and Georgics ,
which are themselves indebted to Lucretius. Vieira discusses Lucan's
dependence on Aen. 8.190-272. Wulff Alonso studies the reception of
Homer, especially of the Iliad, in ancient India; in an appendix, he explores
the possible relationship between the Mababharata and the Aeneid.

The humanists discussed by Bouscharain are Battista Spagnoli (1447-
1516); the printer Josse Bade (beginning of the sixteenth century); and
the court poet Michel ďAmboise (whose translation dates to 1530). II
viaggio d'Enea by Busenello (1598-1659) is a dramatic work. Casanova-
Robin writes about Giovanni Pontano, who wrote Latin eclogues in
the years 1463-1496 on themes borrowed from Vergil and other poets.
Dewar- Watson suggests that Shakespeare's Othello , which alludes to the
Aeneid , may also have been influenced by Montaigne's essay "Of the
Caniballes," which quotes from Aen. 3. Fleck writes on the Life of Vergil
published by Jan van Doesborch (d. 1536) of Antwerp. Galperin considers
the Spanish epic poet Alonso de Ercilla y Zúñiga (1533-1594). Kundert
writes about the reception of Vergil's first, fourth, and ninth Eclogues.
Thomas Murner (1475-ca. 1537), discussed by Redzich, was a German
satirist and translator. Tabárez investigates the authorship of a collation
of the Eclogues wrongly attributed to Juan Luis de la Cerda (1558-1643).
Wallace posits a relationship between Vergil as a theorist of teaching and
the pedagogical practices of Renaissance England.

Cummings discusses the inclusion of Sannazaro's Eclogae piscatoriae
(written in the Vergilian tradition) in late seventeenth-century neo-Latin
anthologies and the attraction this work had for English translators.
Money focuses on the themes of love, names, and politics in Vergilian-
inspired poems written in the period 1685-1714. Trevizam offers an
analysis of the Aeneid translation published by Barreto in the seventeenth
century and how this is mediated by the work of the Portuguese poet
Luís Vaz de Camões.

Thomas writes about Balanchine's Duo concertant and the influence of Vergil's Eclogues on Stravinsky's music for that ballet. Walde posits that a sculptural group from Sperlonga (Museo Archeologico Nazionale, inv. A 1, 10) which includes three piglets may represent the prodigy of the sow described in Aen. 3.389-93 and 8.42-48. Duke University shirley.werner@duke.edu Vergilius 57 (2011) 124
